questions.stem: This special stain of a tissue field highlights slender rod-shaped organisms as pink/red against a blue counterstain. What structural property lets these organisms retain the stain after acid-alcohol decolorization?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A polysaccharide capsule that resists phagocytosis

choices.label: B
choices.text: A thick peptidoglycan layer that traps crystal violet

choices.label: C
choices.text: Teichoic acids anchored in the plasma membrane

choices.label: E
choices.text: An outer membrane rich in lipopolysaccharide

explanation.summary: The acid-fast (Ziehl-Neelsen) stain highlights Mycobacterium tuberculosis because its mycolic-acid-rich waxy cell wall retains carbolfuchsin even after acid-alcohol washing.
explanation.whyCorrect: The acid-fast (Ziehl-Neelsen) stain highlights Mycobacterium tuberculosis because its mycolic-acid-rich waxy cell wall retains carbolfuchsin even after acid-alcohol washing.

questions.stem: This silver-stained tissue section reveals slender black organisms with a tight corkscrew (spiral) shape. What morphologic class of pathogen is shown?

explanation.summary: Silver stains reveal the corkscrew-shaped spirochete Treponema pallidum, the causative agent of syphilis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Silver stains reveal the corkscrew-shaped spirochete Treponema pallidum, the causative agent of syphilis.

questions.stem: These microscopic fields from a syphilitic lesion show inflammation around small arteries with abundant plasma cells and, in the other panel, multinucleated giant cells. Which pair of histologic hallmarks characterizes syphilitic tissue?

explanation.summary: Syphilitic lesions show obliterative endarteritis of small vessels with a dense plasma-cell/lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate, becoming granulomatous (giant cells) in late disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: Syphilitic lesions show obliterative endarteritis of small vessels with a dense plasma-cell/lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate, becoming granulomatous (giant cells) in late disease.

questions.stem: A silver (GMS) stain of this bronchoalveolar lavage specimen highlights dark-gray cyst walls of the causative organism. This infection is classically associated with which radiographic pattern?

explanation.summary: The frothy casts and silver-positive cyst walls identify Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia, a defining opportunistic infection of AIDS. It characteristically produces bilateral interstitial infiltrates with diffuse ground-glass opacities on CT.
explanation.whyCorrect: The frothy casts and silver-positive cyst walls identify Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia, a defining opportunistic infection of AIDS. It characteristically produces bilateral interstitial infiltrates with diffuse ground-glass opacities on CT.

questions.stem: This microscopic field shows a proliferation of bland, spindled cells forming slit-like vascular spaces in a patient with AIDS. Which virus drives this neoplasm?

explanation.summary: Kaposi sarcoma is composed of proliferating spindled endothelial cells and is associated with HHV-8 (also called KSHV). It is a secondary neoplasm of AIDS, presenting as cutaneous, mucosal, or visceral violaceous lesions.
explanation.whyCorrect: Kaposi sarcoma is composed of proliferating spindled endothelial cells and is associated with HHV-8 (also called KSHV). It is a secondary neoplasm of AIDS, presenting as cutaneous, mucosal, or visceral violaceous lesions.

questions.stem: These malignant lymphocytes with enlarged nuclei and intermixed apoptotic bodies constitute an effusion in an AIDS patient. This B-cell malignancy is most associated with which virus?

explanation.summary: Primary effusion lymphoma is an aggressive B-cell lymphoma of body-cavity effusions associated with HHV-8 (often with EBV co-infection). Like other AIDS lymphomas it arises from viral-induced transformation in the setting of immunosuppression.
explanation.whyCorrect: Primary effusion lymphoma is an aggressive B-cell lymphoma of body-cavity effusions associated with HHV-8 (often with EBV co-infection). Like other AIDS lymphomas it arises from viral-induced transformation in the setting of immunosuppression.

questions.stem: Compared with the normal chest radiograph on the left, the film on the right shows a parenchymal focus plus enlarged draining nodes at the lung root. This combination in primary tuberculosis constitutes which classically named complex?

explanation.summary: A peripheral parenchymal Ghon focus together with ipsilateral hilar lymphadenopathy forms the Ghon/Ranke primary complex of primary tuberculosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: A peripheral parenchymal Ghon focus together with ipsilateral hilar lymphadenopathy forms the Ghon/Ranke primary complex of primary tuberculosis.

questions.stem: The tiny arthropods shown beside a coin for scale are the vector responsible for transmitting which infection?

explanation.summary: The image shows the Ixodes (deer) tick, adult and nymph, next to a coin for scale. Ixodes ticks transmit the spirochete Borrelia burgdorferi, the cause of Lyme disease. The nymph is especially important epidemiologically because its small size makes bites easy to miss.
explanation.whyCorrect: The image shows the Ixodes (deer) tick, adult and nymph, next to a coin for scale. Ixodes ticks transmit the spirochete Borrelia burgdorferi, the cause of Lyme disease. The nymph is especially important epidemiologically because its small size makes bites easy to miss.

questions.stem: This gross lung specimen shows the upper apices with softening and cavity formation. In tuberculosis, this apical cavitary pattern is most characteristic of which form of disease?

explanation.summary: A robust immune response in a previously sensitized host causes apical cavitation in the well-oxygenated upper lobes, the hallmark of secondary/reactivation pulmonary tuberculosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: A robust immune response in a previously sensitized host causes apical cavitation in the well-oxygenated upper lobes, the hallmark of secondary/reactivation pulmonary tuberculosis.

questions.stem: The organ on the right is studded with countless tiny granulomatous nodules resembling the millet seeds shown on the left. Which route of spread produces this diffuse seeding pattern?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lymphatic spread to a single node

explanation.summary: Miliary tuberculosis arises from hematogenous dissemination, seeding many organs with innumerable small granulomas that resemble millet seeds.
explanation.whyCorrect: Miliary tuberculosis arises from hematogenous dissemination, seeding many organs with innumerable small granulomas that resemble millet seeds.

questions.stem: This solitary, sharply demarcated ulcer with an indurated clean base appeared painlessly at the inoculation site about three weeks after infection. What is this lesion?

explanation.summary: A single painless ulcer with an indurated clean base at the inoculation site ~3 weeks after infection is the chancre of primary syphilis; chancroid, by contrast, is painful.
explanation.whyCorrect: A single painless ulcer with an indurated clean base at the inoculation site ~3 weeks after infection is the chancre of primary syphilis; chancroid, by contrast, is painful.

questions.stem: This skin shows scaly, papulosquamous plaques in a patient with syphilis. In which stage do these scaling lesions characteristically appear?

explanation.summary: Papulosquamous (scaling) rashes are a manifestation of secondary syphilis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Papulosquamous (scaling) rashes are a manifestation of secondary syphilis.

questions.stem: These images show annular (ring-like) plaques of the face and patches within the oral cavity in secondary syphilis. What is the chief clinical significance of these mucocutaneous lesions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They indicate irreversible tertiary organ damage

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are pathognomonic of HIV coinfection

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are highly infectious, teeming with spirochetes

choices.label: D
choices.text: They represent an allergic drug eruption

explanation.summary: Mucous patches and mucocutaneous lesions of secondary syphilis contain abundant spirochetes and are highly contagious.
explanation.whyCorrect: Mucous patches and mucocutaneous lesions of secondary syphilis contain abundant spirochetes and are highly contagious.

questions.stem: Compared with the normal cord on the left, the special-stained spinal cord on the right shows loss of axons and myelin confined to one region. Which part of the cord degenerates in this form of neurosyphilis?

explanation.summary: Tabes dorsalis, a form of neurosyphilis, degenerates the dorsal columns and dorsal roots, producing sensory ataxia and impaired proprioception.
explanation.whyCorrect: Tabes dorsalis, a form of neurosyphilis, degenerates the dorsal columns and dorsal roots, producing sensory ataxia and impaired proprioception.

questions.stem: These images from a neonate show a maculopapular rash of the feet and perianal moist plaques, appearing within months of birth. These are early manifestations of what condition?

explanation.summary: Transplacental Treponema pallidum causes congenital syphilis; early findings include snuffles, mucocutaneous lesions/condylomata lata, and rash.
explanation.whyCorrect: Transplacental Treponema pallidum causes congenital syphilis; early findings include snuffles, mucocutaneous lesions/condylomata lata, and rash.

questions.stem: This child shows small, notched, peg-shaped upper incisors together with a depressed nasal bridge. The dental abnormality is a hallmark of late congenital syphilis and carries which eponym?

explanation.summary: Notched, peg-shaped incisors (Hutchinson teeth) with saddle nose are late manifestations of congenital syphilis, part of the Hutchinson triad.
explanation.whyCorrect: Notched, peg-shaped incisors (Hutchinson teeth) with saddle nose are late manifestations of congenital syphilis, part of the Hutchinson triad.

questions.stem: This expanding annular skin lesion with a pale central clearing is the classic early manifestation of infection by which organism?

explanation.summary: The bullseye-like annular erythema with central clearing is erythema migrans, the hallmark of early localized Lyme disease caused by the spirochete Borrelia burgdorferi. It typically appears at the tick bite site and resolves over 4-12 weeks.
explanation.whyCorrect: The bullseye-like annular erythema with central clearing is erythema migrans, the hallmark of early localized Lyme disease caused by the spirochete Borrelia burgdorferi. It typically appears at the tick bite site and resolves over 4-12 weeks.

questions.stem: These white, curd-like plaques on the oral mucosa of a patient with advancing HIV infection are caused by which organism?

explanation.summary: Oral candidiasis (thrush) presents as white patches that can be scraped off, reflecting superficial fungal overgrowth of Candida when cell-mediated immunity declines. It is a common early clue to immunosuppression in chronic HIV infection.
explanation.whyCorrect: Oral candidiasis (thrush) presents as white patches that can be scraped off, reflecting superficial fungal overgrowth of Candida when cell-mediated immunity declines. It is a common early clue to immunosuppression in chronic HIV infection.

questions.stem: This severe, deep perirectal ulceration in an AIDS patient is most characteristically caused by reactivation of which organism?

explanation.summary: In advanced immunosuppression, HSV can reactivate to produce chronic, severe perirectal (and oral/esophageal) ulcerations that fail to heal, in contrast to the small self-limited lesions seen in immunocompetent hosts.
explanation.whyCorrect: In advanced immunosuppression, HSV can reactivate to produce chronic, severe perirectal (and oral/esophageal) ulcerations that fail to heal, in contrast to the small self-limited lesions seen in immunocompetent hosts.

questions.stem: In the schematic of disease progression depicted here, which host cell population is progressively destroyed, driving the transition to the final stage shown at the bottom?

explanation.summary: The flow diagram traces HIV from mucosal entry through lymph-node dissemination and viremia to AIDS. Progressive depletion of CD4+ helper T cells is the central event; once the count falls below 200/microL the patient is defined as having AIDS.
explanation.whyCorrect: The flow diagram traces HIV from mucosal entry through lymph-node dissemination and viremia to AIDS. Progressive depletion of CD4+ helper T cells is the central event; once the count falls below 200/microL the patient is defined as having AIDS.
